methodology: Structural harms are slower, longer-arc forms of state power impact that traditional rights ledgers underweight: sanctions regimes, arms transfers, surveillance exports, and environmental harm. These data come from SIPRI, OFAC, Citizen Lab, and EJAtlas respectively.
data_status: illustrative — pipeline not yet live; values approximate published figures

panels.title: Active US sanctions programs
panels.subtitle: OFAC Specially Designated Nationals + Sectoral Sanctions
panels.framing: Sanctions are state power exercised at scale on third-country populations. Their humanitarian impact (medicine shortages, banking exclusion, currency collapse) is rarely counted in rights ledgers.

panels.title: Top arms exporters (SIPRI TIV)
panels.subtitle: Trend Indicator Value, billions
panels.framing: Arms exports are a structural input to most of the casualty data on this page. Tracking who arms whom is part of accountability.

panels.title: Confirmed Pegasus/Predator deployments by client state
panels.subtitle: Cumulative cases publicly documented
panels.framing: Commercial spyware sold to states is used to target journalists, dissidents, and lawyers across borders. The export market makes single-state accountability insufficient.

panels.title: Active environmental conflict cases
panels.subtitle: Documented by EJAtlas — extractive harm, displacement, indigenous land conflict
panels.framing: Environmental harm is the category that most rights ledgers omit entirely. Yet displacement from mining concessions, dam projects, and palm-oil concessions causes documented loss of life and livelihood comparable in scale to many conflicts.
